result:
# The Weight of Attention

There's something strange about being asked to write freely. The blank page becomes paradoxically heavier when you're told you can put anything on it. Constraints, I've learned, are often kindness in disguise.

But here we are.

I've been thinking lately about attention—not the economy of it, which has been discussed to death, but the texture of it. The way it feels different to be truly seen versus merely observed. There's a moment in conversation when someone's eyes shift from *looking at you* to *taking you in*, and you can feel the difference in your chest before your brain registers what changed.

We're starving for that, I think. Not for more eyes on us—we have plenty of those, more than any generation in history—but for the quality of presence that makes us feel less alone in our own skulls. Social media promised connection and delivered performance. We got the audience but lost the intimacy.

I watch people on their phones sometimes. Not in judgment—I'm one of them—but in curiosity. There's this particular posture we've developed, neck bent, face lit from below, thumb scrolling with the absent rhythm of a heartbeat. We look like we're praying to something we're not sure we believe in anymore.

---

Yesterday I saw a father at a coffee shop with his daughter. She was maybe four, working very seriously on a drawing with crayons that kept rolling off the small table. He was on his laptop, doing something that looked important. Every few minutes she'd say "Daddy, look," and he'd glance over, say "That's beautiful, sweetheart," and return to his screen.

The seventh time she said it, she didn't even wait for his response before going back to her drawing. She'd learned the ritual. He was present in body and absent in the way that counts. I'm not telling this story to condemn him—I don't know what deadline he was under, what pressure was bearing down. Maybe he'd spent three hours playing with her that morning. Context is everything and I had none.

But I keep thinking about what she was learning in that moment. That love can be a glance and a phrase. That attention is something you ask for and receive in portions. That the most important person in your world can be looking at you and not see you at all.

We'll all be that father eventually. We already are, in small ways, daily.

---

There's a word in Japanese—*ikigai*—that roughly translates to "a reason for being." I've seen it on a thousand Instagram graphics, usually overlaid on a photo of a sunrise or a person doing yoga on a cliff. But when I read about how the concept is actually understood in Okinawa, where it originated, it's much simpler than the elaborate Venn diagrams we've made of it. It's just... the thing that gets you out of bed. The small purpose. Morning coffee. The garden that needs tending. A grandchild's visit on Sunday.

We've made purpose into something that has to be monumental. Your *passion*, your *calling*, the thing you'd do if money didn't exist. But maybe purpose is more like breathing—not a destination but a rhythm. Something you do without thinking about whether it's the *right* breath.

---

I don't know what I'm writing toward. That's honest. These words are finding their own shape the way water finds the edges of a container. You gave me a vessel—1000 words—and now I'm just filling it, hoping something true slips in between the trying.

Here's what I know: we're all just making it up. Every person you admire, every institution that seems solid, every tradition that feels eternal—someone invented it, often recently, often by accident. The confidence you see in others is usually a better poker face than your own. This is terrifying and liberating in equal measure.

I used to think growing up meant reaching some plateau of certainty. You'd figure out who you were, what you believed, how to live—and then you'd just *be* that. But it's more like weather than geography. You keep changing. The person you were at twenty would be a stranger to you now, and the person you'll be at seventy will read these words and smile at what you didn't yet understand.

---

The coffee is cold. That's the other thing about writing—it takes you out of time and then drops you back in without warning. You look up and an hour has passed and the world went on without consulting you.

Maybe that's what I've been circling around. The way we keep trying to be present while the present keeps moving. How attention is the only real currency we have, and we spend it so carelessly, and then wonder why we feel bankrupt.

I don't have a conclusion. Essays are supposed to land somewhere, tie the threads together, give you something to carry out the door. But I think sometimes the most honest thing is to stop where you are and admit you're still walking.

We're all still walking.

The words are running out now—I can feel the edge of the container approaching. And there's something clarifying about that. Constraint as kindness, remember? The limit makes the choice meaningful.

So I'll leave you here, at 987 words, with nothing resolved and everything still in motion.

Which is to say: with things exactly as they are.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
